After weeks of tension that shook global energy markets, attention is now turning to whether a fragile agreement in the Middle East can prevent a supply shock and keep oil flowing through one of the world's most critical choke points. Joining us now to talk through what this really is happening here and what it means for all of us is James Baycallis, energy reporter at Politico. Thank you so much for being here, James. >> Great to be here. >> Of course, now we have so much to get to here. There is a lot going on in this agreement. Markets finally seem to be breathing a sigh of relief following this provisional agreement. What are the most important details that investors and energy watchers should really be paying attention to right now? >> Well, the most important thing is that the Strait of Hormuz is reopened under the agreement. Um this was what uh the oil industry, what a lot of industries were waiting for for months. Um you know, they they said that regardless of other measures that governments uh and companies were taking, the most important thing was to get the Strait of Hormuz open. And under the agreement, Iran agreed to open the Strait of Hormuz immediately, uh although it will acknowledges that it will take some time to uh remove mines and other obstacles. Uh Iran agreed to do that within 30 days. Uh so, what we've seen since the agreement has been signed, there's definitely been a market increase in traffic through the Strait of Hormuz. It's not back to pre-war levels yet, but it's definitely improving. >> Yeah, I mean, this Strait of Hormuz has been at the center really of all of this for so long now. How close did the world really come to a major disruption? I mean, we know that energy prices did go through the roof, but how close did we come to a serious disruption here? And why is this waterway just so important to global energy security? >> Well, prior to the war, uh 20% of the world's oil passed through this Strait every day, uh not to mention other products like fertilizer, uh natural gas, uh helium, other things that the global economy really relies on. The um you know, even though the Strait of Hormuz was reopened under the agreement, the oil markets are still sort of on a knife's edge. The um uh oil markets didn't go oil prices didn't go quite as high as a a lot of analysts might have expected. There were some projections that we might get to $200 a barrel uh or even higher. Uh in fact, the highest we got to was around $120 a barrel, and most of the war uh oil actually stayed under $100. And a large reason for that was the global inventories that were built up prior to the war. Both companies and governments maintained stockpiles of oil, and uh we were able to draw on those over the last couple of months to prevent prices from going higher. That being said, we're now at a point where a lot of these inventories are reaching what's called tank bottom. Um they there's not much more left that can be drawn down. So, if there's any further disruption, if it takes longer than expected to get oil flowing out of the Middle East again, we could see prices rise uh again. >> Yeah, I mean, so many different factors here. Those stockpiles obviously such an important piece of the puzzle. But I mean, if shipping through this region does fully normalize somewhat soon, how quickly do you think that that would be reflected in energy prices, insurance costs, global supply chains, all of those other things that we're keeping our eyes on right now? >> That's a great question. It's uh we've seen oil prices drop significantly since the agreement was signed. Uh today they're around $73 a barrel, which is um getting close to where they were before the war started. Um the uh gas prices in the US, however, still remain pro- about a dollar higher than they were before the war started. That typically takes a few days to weeks to reflect any changes in the oil prices. I think that the in terms of the long-term impacts though, this has really shifted a lot of people's mindsets about supply chains particularly for oil, but also some of those other products that I mentioned. Oil companies, oil governments are now thinking about ways to diversify their supplies, whether that means going to other types of energy like coal or renewables, or finding new ways to get oil out of the Middle East like new pipelines that go around the Strait of Hormuz. >> Yeah, I mean people are really now scrambling trying to think about what they can do in the future to prepare if anything like this happens again. And as we look to the future, I mean there's been so much coverage and so much talk on the Strait of Hormuz, the ceasefire itself. But what are the biggest really unresolved questions or disagreements that could still really create new problems or continued problems down the road? >> One of the big ones is definitely fees. Iran has threatened repeatedly throughout the war and since even since the agreement's been signed that they plan to charge fees for ships transiting the Strait of Hormuz going forward. Now, the Trump administration has said that's not acceptable and the oil industry has also opposed any new fees or tolls. But the agreement, the MOU, it bars Iran from charging any fees for 60 days, but after that it leaves the administration of the Strait of Hormuz up to Iran and Oman which lies on the other side of the Strait. And those two countries today uh put out um a statement that said they're looking at implementing a fee system. So, that that could be another flashpoint going forward. Um, especially President Trump over the weekend said that uh uh again said that Iran uh will not be charging any fees. So, that's uh definitely a point of disagreement that's yet to be fully resolved. >> Yeah, I mean this debate over fees now from multiple different countries is going to have a huge impact in the area. Remains to be seen what that actually will be, but if those do come to fruition um after that 60-day mark, then we will see. When we talk about this cost of an agreement like this, what are we really talking about? Is it sanctions relief, security guarantees, reconstruction, military commitments? Which of those could really have the biggest long-term impact here? >> Yeah, I think that uh you know, in terms of what we're watching in the energy space, uh this this crisis created this fundamental shift in people's mindset where um countries are going to have to be are are thinking more about um their the security of their energy supplies. Um, I've heard a lot in in uh the weeks since the war began and even just in the last couple of days that countries are looking at creating new stockpiles of oil um like our strategic petroleum reserve. Um, that could fundamentally sort of shift how oil markets work. Um, and in the long run, um I think that a lot of the industry is watching um is expecting that shipping costs are going to remain higher uh for the foreseeable future. Um, insurance costs have gone up um in particular because of the threats of the Strait of Hormuz. Um, that doesn't seem like it's eased yet. Um, and also um shippers shipping costs just around the world have been higher because of all the disruptions. There tankers stuck in the Middle East, and uh and and the global supply chains are going to take a significant amount of time to recover. >> Yeah, I mean so many different factors at play here that we have to keep our eye on. When we look towards the US energy policy, you mentioned this a little bit about stockpiles, what could this agreement really do to influence energy policy here at home, particularly as Washington balances domestic production with strategic reserves, and then also its role in the global energy market overall as well? >> Yeah, the United States played a really key role during this crisis in preventing oil prices from going higher. You know, a lot of analysts credit the fact that really the US shale boom has allowed and record US oil production has meant that we were less reliant on imports, and we were also able to supply the world, make up for some of these supply losses. So, that's an important lesson I think that the US is going to take from this, that it's now able, you know, with this record oil production to really be a supplier for the world in a time of emergency. That being said, I don't think the US is immune from Definitely this crisis has shown the US is not immune from the global oil market disruptions and the increase in oil prices we saw. So, there was the American Petroleum Institute, the top US oil lobby, yesterday put out a series of recommendations for sort of lessons they've taken from this crisis. One of them being to rebuild and really invest in the strategic petroleum reserve. And another key recommendation they made was solidifying the waiver process under the Jones Act, which would allow more goods to travel between US ports. Um, that's a a controversial um, that's been long controversial and uh, now the oil industry is taking the position that there needs to be some more certainty in in the waiver process for that law. >> Well, we have a lot to keep our eye on here. There's so many different factors that you've helped us break down. So, we really appreciate your time. James MacAlias, energy reporter at Politico, thank you so much for being here today. >> Great to be here. Thanks.

As advances in bionics, AI, and robotics continue to transform industries and improve lives, we explore how one company is bridging human ability and machine intelligence. Joining us now to talk all about it is Dr. Aydin Aktar, founder and CEO of Psyonic. Thank you so much for being here. >> Thank you for having me. Hello. >> [laughter] >> Of course, hello there. This is the best greeting I've gotten on here before. That's incredible. So, for viewers who may not be familiar with Psyonic, I know you're already giving us a little bit of a preview now, but tell us a little bit about the company and the mission that inspired you to found it. >> Yeah, so what we're doing at Psyonic is we are developing and solving dexterity for humans and robots. And we do that through both bionic hardware, like the ability hand that I'm holding here, as well as AI software. And what's unique about us is that we can actually train robots to do all sorts of different tasks based on how our human users use the do the same task with the same hand. And so, I personally have wanted to build bionic limbs since I was 7. My parents are from Pakistan. I was visiting, and that was the first time I met someone missing a limb. She was my age missing her right leg using a tree branch as a crutch. And that's what inspired me to go into this field and build bionic limbs that are accessible for both humans and robots. >> Yeah, that's such an incredible story and so amazing to be inspired at such a young age and then to actually do something about it. That's just amazing. Um you guys recently announced a partnership with ABB Robotics centered around this ability hand. Is that what you're holding there? >> That that is exactly right. Yeah. >> Okay, that's what I thought. Yeah, tell us a little bit more about that collaboration. And it really seems like it's first of its kind with this kind of partnership between prosthetics potentially, robotics, all these things coming together. >> And yeah, and it turns out that if you're building a robot to do human tasks, we've optimized our hands for humans to do human tasks as a prosthetic hand. And so, our human users, they've figured out how to do like everything from like washing dishes to folding laundry to tying shoelaces to industrial pick and place tasks to stocking shelves. And the whole idea is is that on the robotic side, you can move your robot from point A to point B, but they haven't been able to do anything useful. And because our human users have been able to figure out how to do the useful things, they're training the robots by taking the data that's generated from their hand and then using that to build AI models then to train the robots to do the exact same tasks. >> Yeah, that's just incredible to think about how you can kind of bridge that gap between these two worlds that really do make a lot of sense together that maybe people hadn't thought of before as really being integral parts to each other. So, tell us a little bit more about this ability hand. It looks like it's amazing and an incredible feat of engineering there. How does the technology actually work and what makes it different from other prosthetic solutions that are on the market? >> Yeah, and so there's six motors in here. So, all five fingers flex and extend. The thumb rotates as well. And so, it's FDA-approved. It's covered by Medicare. So, it's very affordable here in the US. But also, it is the first prosthetic hand to give users touch feedback. So, our very first human user in the US, he Sergeant Garrett Anderson is lost his hand in Iraq in 2005 due to a roadside bomb. He told us he could actually feel his daughter's hand and that was something that he wasn't able to do with any other prosthetic hand. And to be able to take that information and translate that over to the robotic side, right? Where robots can then interact with humans or for example, grab raspberries without crushing them or do all sorts of delicate movements in a workplace setting. That's what we're really unlocking with the ability hand for both humans and robots. >> Yeah, and it's just incredible to think about all of these capabilities that you're giving humans. Like you mentioned, someone who might be amputated, as well as giving robots to be able to then help humans in the future. Tell us a little bit about some of the challenges that you must face because I cannot imagine how difficult this must be. Tell us about some of the challenges and how you overcome those in all of your work. >> Yeah, and I mean one of the biggest challenges right now in robotics has been dexterity, right? Getting the the robots to do useful things. And and so by having our human users do a lot of those those tasks, we can actually get like the industrial pick and place tasks to to work. So for example, when our human users control the hand, right? They'll use their muscles to to make it move. They'll they'll grab an object. But it's not as simple as just like grabbing the object. We need to sync it to cameras, for example. So they'll wear Meta Ray-Ban glasses. And that way we know exactly what the task is. We can sync it to like when they were grabbing the object. We can measure how hard they were grabbing the object. And then when we have that camera data, we can put a camera on the work cell at the at an ABB installed environment and an industrial setting and then have the robots do the same task. >> Yeah, I mean hearing again just how you can integrate these two concepts and actually make both better is really just amazing. You know, we're hearing more about this concept of physical AI. So tell us a little bit more about what that term actually means to you and how Psyonic's work with NVIDIA's AI training platform is actually helping advance this field. >> Yeah, and so the thing is is that one of the biggest things that that have unlocked like robots to do all sorts of amazing things recently is all the advances in AI that that have been happening. And so we started this partnership with NVIDIA where we use their entire AI stack from Isaac Lab to Groot to take in the data from the the ability hand when human users are are are using it and then going through that software stack to train the robots in simulation and then in the real world to have them do the same tasks. And NVIDIA has been an incredible partner to us in enabling that whole workflow to happen. >> Yeah, it seems like these partnerships are so important with different people working on kind of different aspects of this industry and how you can really put it together to solve the problem that you are trying to solve here. Tell us a little bit about some of the broader industry implications of this kind of convergence between human-centered assistive technology and next-generation robotics because it really does seem like you're you're kind of bridging the gap and bringing a lot of different elements together here. >> Absolutely. And our goal is seamless integration between humans and robots. And so we recently got a a grant from the US military to develop microwire electrodes that you can plant in your nerves to get really fine dextrous control of the hand. But then also when you touch the fingers right now, you feel a vibration. That lets you know how hard you're pressing. But with the nerve implants, we can stimulate the nerves and make the sensation feel like it's actually coming from your missing hand. And unlocking that for humans is going to be critical because then it feels like it's an extension of your body as opposed to just being a tool that that's at the the end of your body. And by unlocking the dexterity for humans, we can then in turn unlock it for the robots, too. So what we build for humans benefits robots. And then the volumes on the robot side can subsidize the cost for humans so that more people can afford bionic limbs. So we build for robots benefits humans. It's an incredible ecosystem that benefits both industrial manufacturing and humanity worldwide. >> Yeah, it does really seem like again, they just go together so well here. Tell us a little bit more about that dexterity piece of it and how that that nerve part of that works cuz that's just fascinating to me. I think we can all imagine a robot, you know, picking something up or a hand doing something like that. But actually having the nerve part integrated so that there's feeling, how does that really work? >> Yeah. Yeah. And so the thing is is right when when we're controlling our own natural hands, right? We're we're basically telling it to uh you know, you can move each individual finger. But when you're grasping an object, like if I'm grasping this this cup right over here, right? I'm not really thinking about like, oh, I'm placing my fingers here. I'm thinking about how much pressure I'm applying to this so that it the the the uh mug doesn't drop, right? So, I can grab this with the hand and I can feel through the vibration exactly how much um force that I'm applying on this. But, it still feels like a vibration. To really unlock that to feel like it's it's your own natural hand, we need to basically trick your brain into thinking that the the hand is your own. And by stimulating your nerves, it sends that signal back to your brain to make it actually feel like it's coming from your missing hand that isn't there anymore. We've actually done this with brain implants before with uh collaborators at the University of Pittsburgh where they had uh people who were paralyzed in car accidents um and they installed brain implants in them and they could wirelessly control each finger. But, then they blindfolded them and touched the fingers and they could detect which finger was being touched because it was stimulating that their brain in that area. And this is the future that we're heading in, right? The seamless integration between humans and robots. >> Wow, this is incredible. We really appreciate you being here today and talking through all of this with us. I can't wait to see what happens with this and how this can be applied in so many different ways throughout the world. So, I deal Akhtar, founder and CEO of Psyonic, thank you so much again for talking through this all with us and for sharing the hand with us. Thank you. >> Thank you so much.

The New York City skyline, home to some of the tallest buildings in the Western Hemisphere. Views that generated $85 billion in tourism economic impact in 2025 alone. Empire State Building, Top of the Rock, Summit One Vanderbilt, One World Observatory, Edge NYC. These observation decks used to be simple. You paid, you went up, you looked through coin-operated binoculars, and then left. But today, the race to the top has never been more competitive. >> This is art. This is an observation deck. This is a culinary experience. I think this is a form of entertainment. >> In this episode of NYC Innovates, we're taking a trip 1,100 ft in the sky, exploring the growth of the city's most popular observation decks. We'll discover why Hudson Yards just invested millions of dollars to reimagine New York's most photographed views, and find out if these immersive new experiences are more than just a social media opportunity. >> You just see the reaction from people coming off the elevator. It's this this like wow moment. >> Stay with us. Long before New York was selling skyline views at $40 a ticket, the idea of paying to go up was born across the Atlantic. The concept of the observation deck as a tourist attraction can be traced back to a French engineer named Gustave Eiffel. When he built the Eiffel Tower for the 1889 World's Fair in Paris, he combined cutting-edge elevator technology with lofty viewing platforms. When it [music] opened, 2 million people showed up in the first 6 months, creating an architectural icon designed to generate revenue through public admission. >> [music] >> Just 1 year later, New York City brought that idea home, embedding it into the city's own skyline. The World Building on Park Row was built with the observation deck intentionally designed within the structure. When it was completed in 1890, this 309-ft tower was not only the tallest building in New York, but also the tallest office building in the world. Its observation deck served as a massive public draw, offering unobstructed panoramic city views for 5 cents. Then came the skyscraper that changed everything. In 1931, the Empire State Building opened, an architectural flex in the depths of the Great Depression. New Yorkers called it the Empty State Building because the offices all sat vacant. But the cramped observation deck was a success. What nobody predicted was that the view would turn out to be worth more than the address itself. Today, the observation decks on the 86th and 102nd floors draw nearly 3 million visitors a year, generating $136 million in annual revenue. Top of the Rock opened just 2 years later in 1933, drawing 1,300 visitors a day. The Twin Towers added a deck in 1975, but following the tragedy of 9/11, it took 14 years for New York to reclaim a view from downtown when One World's Observatory finally opened in 2015. Then, as a new generation of visitors arrived with smartphones in hand and Instagram feeds to fill, the industry couldn't build fast enough. Edge at 30 Hudson Yards in 2020, Summit One Vanderbilt in 2021, three major decks in 6 years. Today, the observation deck industry has gone from novelty to full-blown competition. The view is no longer enough. These experiences now feature theatrical elevator rides, immersive art installations, photo moments, bars, restaurants, and merchandising. And this year marks the next chapter in that evolution. Hudson Yards invested millions of dollars to build a one-of-a-kind guest experience inside Edge, the highest outdoor observation deck in the Western Hemisphere. >> You want to give people the best experience from the minute they step in to the minute they leave. Especially in this day and age, where people are tied to their phone and pulled apart, they're really looking for great experiences. >> The result, seven permanent indoor environments, each one drawing from the energy, color, and sound of New York City itself. To build something genuinely new, Hudson Yards brought in three studios with very different signatures: Moment Factory, Journey, and Softlab. The common thread, they all understood New York. >> This is a love letter to New York. And so, we spoke to a lot of different agencies, I think over 30, trying to find studios and artists who understood like what the vision was. About taking New York, um the energy, the colors, the we're a melting pot, right? But in a new way. And so, how can we find things that that highlighted what we all love about being a New Yorker. We want things that are able to fully immerse, um and we think that screens only have are two-day. Um and so, we wanted things that are real, people could touch, people could see, people could see the reflection that changes as the time of day. You can never replicate a physical experience. >> Softlab, a design studio based in Chelsea, was selected to build four of the seven environments. >> I think one of the main things that we really kind of focused on when we started working with the larger team, is there's a tons of ticketed experiences that take advantage of flat media, uh digital media, LED screens, projection mapping, [music] and things like that. So, we wanted to bring the qualities of those things like interactivity, dynamic lighting, but also make it much more spatial, three-dimensional, and immersive. So, we did four immersive installations, and all of them combine materiality, um space, lighting, and interactivity to make a much more, I'd say, immersive, tactile experience. >> The installation starts before you even reach the observation deck with pulse. >> Pulse on level four, um is 450 individually programmed uh light orbs, and has nooks for guests to kind of gather before as they're queuing. So, that one's also interactive. Each time someone steps into a nook, uh a pulse of white light, which is almost you can envision as an expanding circle, um happens. So, when there are multiple guests, it's almost like ripples in a pond. So, the idea is guests work together to produce this kind of idiosyncratic symphony of color, light, and and sounds. That one how it almost appears as worlds within worlds like New York City. It's also about being interconnected in many ways. >> After the elevators open, you're welcomed into Crystal Cave, a 25-ft canopy of hanging crystals, each one engineered to catch light and cast it across the floor in constantly shifting rainbows. >> Here you're in a place where you don't really get shadows from other buildings. So, I think that was something we could really take advantage of is the sunlight as a activator of all the installations. >> From there, enter the Kaleidoscope, described as New York City's largest. The installation pulls guests through a series of rooms where light and color shift in real time, set to original music. Young Americans are redefining what it means to earn a living with millions turning to side hustles, content creation, and freelance work as traditional 9-5 employment becomes just one of many pathways to financial success. Um the modern earner, uh about 117 million Americans, about like one in three Americans, um they are significantly different in how they earn because they're not uh generally earning through the traditional paycheck, right? The traditional W-2. They're earning through multiple sources of income. They have variable income streams. Um you can often think of them as the gig platform worker, the freelancer, the creator. Um and fundamentally, what that means is on top of a W-2, they may be earning uh an independent um earning stream. They could also be an hourly worker. But what all of these people have in common is that their income is very variable and inconsistent. And as a result of that, it's more difficult to give them the recognition that they need in the financial system to give them the access to the credit and the benefits that ordinarily would be much easier for someone receiving a W-2. >> Yeah, I mean, those changes and that kind of overall shift is something that we're really seeing now across all sectors, right? With people from all different sectors really trying to look at these kind of side hustles. Tell us a little bit more about this independent income source. So, what's driving so many young people to seek this income outside of a traditional job. >> Well, I think a lot of things have changed technology wise and structurally. So, what we've seen is that technology has enabled so many more people to be able to take what usually required a corporate job and access to uh to be able to be doing that simply from your phone, right? Like you could be a creator making $50,000 a year to $100,000 a year simply just using your phone itself. So, the accessibility and the technology is enabling a lot of these shifts. Um we're also seeing that Gen Zs and younger folks are more likely to be self-motivated through the flexibility of being able to earn your own income versus being tied to traditional 9-5. And then obviously with a lot of the AI pushes recently, we're seeing a lot of the AI enabled independent native work streams happening where you can leverage AI to be multiple different people within your team and you can actually leverage your identity and your interest to pursue what you might want to do and monetize that. >> Yeah, I mean that makes a lot of sense that technology is just making all of this even more possible than it ever was before. I mean thinking about the part of your report where you talk about independent earner population growing 30% in just 2 years. I mean, how significant is that shift and what does that really tell us about the future of work overall? >> Well, I think this change has been happening for quite a while now and I think this continued shift has only continued to accelerate. I think what it fundamentally means is that more and more people are going to be earning in these much more flexible formats. And what that means financially is that we have to figure out ways that we can honor that flexibility, recognize that flexibility and give benefits, financial benefits to Um, these type of flexible workers in a meaningful way that gives them access to credit, gives them access to easier access to mortgage, easier access to be able to do their taxes, and really help them bridge that financial variability so they can make rent and general regular payments. >> Yeah, that's so important because again, it is so difficult to then figure out how to, you know, use all of the financial tools that are out there now for people with the typical 9-5 for this now new style of work. So, again, so important when we think about all of those different pieces coming together. When you think about this kind of side hustle that people were often using, you know, earning extra money for spending money, things like that. Are you seeing more young people now treat these income streams as their primary source of earnings? So, it's no longer just for extra money on the side, but really their primary source? >> I think um, right now we're still still seeing a good balance of multiple income streams. So, whether it's a W-2 plus an independent hustle on the side. I think over time what we'll see is more and more comfort with people potentially being able to lean more on their independent work stream as the primary source of income. But for now, the patterns that we're seeing is most of the time we're seeing multiple sources of income with one of the income sources being a W-2. >> Yeah, and and when it comes to the banking aspect of all of this, I mean, one of the most striking findings is that 64% of modern earners define their primary bank as where they manage and spend their money, not where their paycheck is actually deposited. So, why has that definition changed? Talk to us a little bit more about that piece of things. >> Well, I think that's something really unique to the insights that we have found at Cash App. What we found is that so many of these modern earners are on Cash App. So, as we've been studying all the behavioral patterns on Cash App, what we're seeing is that um, so many of them think of Cash App as their primary bank, right? But a lot of them actually don't earn the traditional direct deposits. So, what we've been seeing is where they actually think of their top-of-wallet spending behavior, right? What they use to spend on a daily basis is the biggest signifier for primary banking for a lot of these um independent earners and modern earners. And what we've been doing is creating products to help uh support that behavior and honor that primary banking uh perception and and behavior. >> Yeah, it seems like that trend of actually using Cash App and kind of having that be in a way their primary financial institution right now. Um what does that really say about how this younger generation is viewing banking? Again, not just with how they're viewing work now and not having that be traditional, but how they're also viewing banking. >> Yeah, I think um the younger generation has a lot of choices. So, um as they become much more flexible in how they're earning, they're going to need money uh much more instantly, right? Uh they're also going to need to um understand that their bank can see how they're moving their money in a way that feels personalized to them. Um they're going to want to have better credit access, right? And they're going to want to have better ways to manage all of these multiple income streams.
